Author: Tero Pikala
Title: Vibe coding with JetBrains AI agent Junie
Text:
Vibe coding with JetBrains AI agent Junie
I wanted to try vibe coding with an AI agent since it’s been such a hot topic lately. When deciding what to build, I knew it had to be something reasonably quick and safe.
That gave me a chance to combine it with another one of my passions: DIY. Over the years, I’ve collected quite a few Makita power tools. I’ve got so many packed in Makpac cases that it’s actually hard to pick the right one at a glance.
There are people selling labels on eBay and Etsy, but I couldn’t find a decent template for printing my own — so I figured others might find a solution like this useful too.
In the end, I spent less than 10 hours building a tool that can generate labels like this:
I started by defining the overall project and architecture in a short Markdown file:
## Background
This project is a website to generate labels for Makita Makpac tool
cases. Users can select the correct case size and tool model to
initially populate the dialogs. There's also space for custom
details like owners' name and contact details.
Information entered is only saved to cookies, and there is no backend
implementation.
## Architecture and tooling
React: Front end components
Tailwind CSS: Styling
pdf-lib: PDF generation
## Deployment
Application code is deployed into AWS S3 bucket and served via
Amazon CloudFront CDN.
## Features
1. Select MakPac case type (3 options)
2. Select a Makita tool
3. Enter optional custom details
4. Download generated PDF
Clearly, using React and Tailwind for something simple like this is overkill but I also wanted refresh my skills with those a bit. Pdf-lib was chosen because it seemed to have good features to generate PDF programmatically.
Junie created the initial project structure and pretty much nailed all the requirements I gave it.
The only issue was the Tailwind CSS setup, which was broken and prevented the app from starting. After a few failed attempts to fix it (with AI help and manually), I gave up and just installed Tailwind manually using the official docs.
Another challenge for Junie was getting rounded corners in the label template. Using SVGs made the most sense, but describing exactly what I wanted in a way that AI could understand wasn’t so straightforward.
In the end, I created the SVG path myself and manually positioned it in the corners.
I wanted to deploy the code to an AWS S3 bucket and handle SSL using Nginx Proxy Manager. Since there’s no backend, this setup gave me solid security, easy deployments, and quick SSL certificate installation with Let’s Encrypt.
Junie helped a lot here — it generated instructions and config for deployment, which saved me a bit of time.
The whole experience was exciting, surprising — and a little bit scary. I’m still wrapping my head around how well AI can understand what I want and often provide usable solutions out of the box.
I was especially impressed with how easily it handled PDF generation. With just a few extra prompts, I was able to add logo, disclaimers, and even crop marks.
That said, using a coding agent like Junie does make you a bit lazy. I didn’t bother to inspect all parts of the code too deeply because, well, it just worked. That’s great for speed, but not so much for really understanding the tools you’re using.
Check out the results in https://caselabel.pikala.com/ and if you want to check the code see https://github.com/teropikala/caselabel
